version_a.subject_line: something we noticed at masonic villages

emails.email_body:
Hi Justin,

Noticed Masonic Villages is running operations across multiple CCRC campuses in Pennsylvania with a team of 2500. That kind of footprint means billing and coding complexity across a lot of payer mixes, care settings, and locations.

One VP at a similar senior living network was managing the same sprawl and cut A/R days by 30% after moving to autonomous coding — without adding to their billing team.

VPs use RapidClaims to reduce coding costs by 40% while keeping accuracy above 98%. Worth asking whether that math holds for an operation Masonic Villages' size?

Hey Justin,

Since I mentioned that senior living network, here's what the numbers actually looked like. They were stretched across four campuses, dealing with inconsistent coder output and rising cost-to-collect. After deploying RapidClaims in about 8 weeks, their coders were handling 1.7x the charts per day with no additional headcount.

I can share a simple benchmark we use with multi-site operators — it maps coding cost against campus count and payer mix to show where the drag usually lives.

Would that kind of framework be useful to pressure-test against what you're seeing across your campuses?

Hello Justin,

Most operations leaders we talk to at networks your size are focused on headcount and margin. The thing that usually surprises them is how much revenue leakage is sitting in denials, not staffing. Masonic Villages' scale means that problem compounds across every campus.

VPs use RapidClaims to cut denials by 27% and bring cost-to-collect down by 70%, typically within a single quarter of going live.

Worth 15 minutes to walk through what that looks like for a CCRC network specifically?

version_b.subject_line: the masonic villages billing question we keep hearing

emails.email_body:
Hi Justin,

Here's something that keeps coming up with multi-campus senior living operators: the organizations that look the most operationally mature on paper are usually the ones quietly absorbing the most billing drag. More locations, more payer contracts, more places for revenue to slip.

Masonic Villages is running 2500 people across multiple Pennsylvania campuses. That's a lot of moving parts to keep revenue cycle clean.

Curious, when you look across your campuses, is the billing complexity something your team is actively managing or something that's mostly running in the background?

Hey Justin,

Since I raised that question about billing drag at scale, here's what actually happened at one org that looked a lot like Masonic Villages. Multi-site senior living network, operationally strong, assumed their RCM was fine because it wasn't visibly broken.

They ran a benchmark and found their coders were processing roughly half the charts per day of what autonomous coding could handle. Deploying RapidClaims in 8 weeks got them to 1.7x coder productivity and cut their cost-to-collect by 70%.

The drag was there the whole time. They just hadn't had a number to put on it. Want me to share how that benchmark works?

Hello Justin,

One thing I didn't mention earlier: the network I referenced wasn't struggling. They were hitting their targets, keeping staff in place, no visible fires. The 70% cost-to-collect improvement and 27% denial reduction came on top of a baseline that already looked healthy.

That's usually what we find at organizations Masonic Villages' size. It's not about fixing something broken, it's about finding what scale is quietly costing you.

I'd like to show you what that looks like for a CCRC operator specifically. Would 15 minutes this week work?

pairs.archetype_description: 4 sentences max, 55-70 words. Problem → Solution → Proof → CTA with no narrative.

version_a.subject_line: your new role at hospice of the valley

emails.email_body:
Hi Connie,

Saw your post about stepping into the VP of Business Operations role at Hospice of the Valley. New role, new ownership over where the revenue cycle holds or quietly bleeds.

We just wrapped work with a hospice network in a similar spot and their new operations lead found the coding layer was the main drag on A/R days. They ended up pulling two planned billing hires after seeing what automation could cover instead.

VPs use RapidClaims to cut A/R days by 30% in the first quarter. Is RCM something you're evaluating as you get up to speed?

Hey Connie,

Read that Hospice of the Valley is expanding into Tucson and participating in the new Medicare GUIDE model for dementia care. That's a meaningful lift in complexity for the ops and billing side.

One VP at a comparable hospice network told us the GUIDE model billing requirements alone were enough to stress-test their existing coding setup. They got to 98% accuracy and 1.7x coder productivity without adding headcount.

Worth a quick look at whether the same math applies here as you're scaling?

Hello Connie,

Read that Hospice of the Valley had roughly a 12% retirement rate in 2024 and has been navigating hiring challenges on the clinical side. That kind of turnover puts real pressure on the back office too.

VPs in similar positions have used RapidClaims to cut coding costs by 40% while keeping accuracy above 98%, which gives the team room to absorb capacity gaps without just adding headcount.

If it's useful, I can share how a comparable network handled this. Is there a 20-minute window that works?

Hi Connie,

Hospice networks your size typically carry A/R drag that sits in the coding layer, not the billing team. RapidClaims automates clinical coding so your ops team isn't dependent on coder capacity to close claims. A comparable hospice network cut A/R days by 30% in the first quarter after switching. Worth a 20-minute look as you're getting oriented in the role?

Hey Connie,

Expanding into new care models like GUIDE means more coding complexity on the same team. RapidClaims handles that complexity at the source so accuracy doesn't slip when volume grows. One VP at a similar network hit 98% accuracy and 1.7x coder productivity without adding headcount. Is that a problem worth solving right now?

Hello Connie,

High retirement rates in clinical staff create real downstream pressure on coding throughput and collections. RapidClaims fills that gap by processing up to 500 charts per day autonomously, deployed in 8 weeks. A comparable organization cut cost to collect by 70% without a single new hire. Happy to share the specifics if it's relevant to what you're building.

version_a.subject_line: centracare's billing capacity after the restructuring

emails.email_body:
Hi Sherri,

Read that CentraCare cut 535 positions earlier this year, with 70% coming from administrative and support roles including billing. That's a significant reduction in coding and revenue cycle capacity at a time when the system is already working through a $19.8M operating loss.

The tension that creates is real: fewer hands, same volume, same revenue targets.

VPs use RapidClaims to close that gap without rebuilding headcount. One health system reduced A/R days by 30% after a similar restructuring.

How are you thinking about maintaining throughput on the coding side with a leaner team?

Hey Sherri,

Following up on my note from last week.

The piece that tends to surprise VPs in your position is how quickly the math shifts. Coding cost drops 40% and accuracy stays above 98%, so it's not a tradeoff between speed and quality.

For a system CentraCare's size, that kind of efficiency doesn't require a rip-and-replace. Most teams are fully deployed in about 8 weeks.

Worth a 20-minute call to walk through what this looks like against your current capacity model?

Hello Sherri,

Last note from me on this.

With the restructuring behind you and revenue performance still on the line, the gap between available coding capacity and claim volume is the variable that compounds quietly. Denials climb, A/R stretches, and the pressure lands squarely in revenue services.

RapidClaims is purpose-built for exactly that scenario. Coders become 1.7x more productive and cost to collect drops 70%, without adding headcount.

If the timing makes sense, happy to show you how other VP Revenue leaders are running this.

version_b.subject_line: what a midwest health system did after cutting their billing team

emails.email_body:
Hi Sherri,

A VP Revenue Services at a regional Midwest health system laid off a significant portion of her billing staff last year as part of a broader cost reduction. Her concern wasn't the cuts themselves, it was whether first-pass rates would hold after.

They didn't just hold. A/R days dropped 30% within two quarters because autonomous coding removed the backlog her reduced team had been quietly accumulating.

Saw that CentraCare went through a similar restructuring, 535 positions, 70% administrative and billing. Curious whether maintaining revenue throughput is the challenge you're navigating right now?

Hey Sherri,

Different angle, same problem. A CFO at a comparable health system tried to patch their post-layoff coding gap with contract coders. The cost was manageable at first, but accuracy was inconsistent and denials crept up 15% over six months.

They switched to autonomous coding mid-year. Accuracy went above 98%, denial rate dropped 27%, and they were able to let the contract arrangements lapse entirely.

The contrast he described was straightforward: contract coders add variable cost, autonomous coding reduces fixed cost while improving consistency.

Is that tradeoff something you're weighing right now at CentraCare?

Hello Sherri,

Bringing this back to CentraCare specifically.

You're carrying a $19.8M operating loss, a leaner billing team, and the same revenue targets. The VPs who've navigated that combination most successfully didn't try to rehire their way out of it, they changed what each coder could process. Productivity goes to 1.7x, cost to collect drops 70%, and deployment runs about 8 weeks.

That's a meaningful shift in what your revenue services org can do with the team it has.

Would a 20-minute conversation be worth it to see if the numbers hold for CentraCare's volume?

version_a.subject_line: ohsu's operating losses and the coding layer

emails.email_body:

Read about OHSU's $110M operating loss for fiscal 2024 and the S&P downgrade. At that scale, expenses outpacing revenue isn't just a budget problem, it's a signal that every recoverable dollar in the cycle matters.

One VP Finance at a comparable academic medical center ran autonomous coding across their inpatient volume and pulled A/R days down 30% without adding headcount. That kind of recovery at the coding layer compounds quickly against an operating gap.

Is the revenue cycle being re-evaluated as part of the cost reduction work?

Hey Maulin,

Following up on this. The piece that tends to surprise finance leadership is how fast it deploys, typically eight weeks to full production, so the impact hits within the same fiscal year rather than a future budget cycle.

VPs at systems your size are also seeing coder productivity run at 1.7x and accuracy stay above 98%, which means you're not trading throughput for compliance exposure.

Given where OHSU's numbers landed in FY24, that timeline seems relevant. Worth a 20-minute conversation to see if the math holds at your volume?

Hello Maulin,

Last note on this. Saw OHSU's cost-to-revenue trend has been under pressure for multiple years now, not just FY24. That kind of sustained gap usually means the fixes being explored are structural, not tactical.

Autonomous coding cuts cost to collect by up to 70% and doesn't require renegotiating vendor contracts or restructuring teams. It's a different category of lever than what most finance reviews are looking at.

Happy to share what the model looks like at $4B-plus revenue volume if that's a useful input for anything you're working through right now.

emails.email_body:
Hi Amy,

Read that Saint Luke's hospital-at-home program is pushing toward budget-neutrality and seeking a CMS extension. That's a real financial engineering problem — a new care model that has to prove it pays for itself before it scales.

At $3.5B revenue, the margin on that math gets tight fast. One VP Finance at a similar regional health system found the coding layer was quietly bleeding revenue on exactly these newer visit types, where documentation patterns don't fit legacy workflows.

VPs use RapidClaims to cut coding costs by 40% and bring denials down 27%. Is the coding layer part of what's being evaluated as the program expands?

Hey Amy,

Following up on the hospital-at-home thread. One thing that tends to surprise VPs at health systems this size is how much coding throughput variance shows up across service lines — accuracy stays high on core inpatient, but newer programs drift.

The VP Finance I mentioned ran a quick benchmark across their ambulatory and home-based lines. They found coders were processing roughly half the charts per day on newer visit types compared to traditional encounters. That gap compounds fast at scale.

RapidClaims coders process 500 charts per day at 98%+ accuracy across visit types. Worth knowing whether that benchmark holds at Saint Luke's?

Hello Amy,

Last note on this. The VP Finance I mentioned earlier ended up pulling two planned coding hires after the benchmark ran. The full picture: 40% reduction in coding costs, A/R days down 30% in the first quarter, and the hospital-at-home line finally penciling out on its own.

For a program trying to prove budget-neutrality to CMS, that's not a nice-to-have. It's the difference between a pilot that gets extended and one that doesn't.

If it's useful, I can pull together a quick comparison against what Saint Luke's is currently running. Worth a conversation?

version_b.subject_line: a question about saint luke's revenue cycle

emails.email_body:
Hi Amy,

Read about Saint Luke's hospital-at-home program pushing toward budget-neutrality. That's a specific kind of financial pressure — proving a new care model pays for itself before CMS commits to a 5-year extension.

Here's what I'm curious about: a regional health system at roughly the same scale ran that same analysis and found a gap they weren't looking for — not in operations, not in staffing. It was somewhere most finance leaders don't think to look first.

I won't bury the lead forever. But I'm curious — when Saint Luke's models budget-neutrality for the program, what's the biggest variable that feels hardest to control right now?

Hey Amy,

Since I mentioned that health system finding a gap they weren't expecting — here's what actually happened.

Their finance team had assumed coding accuracy was consistent across service lines. It wasn't. Home-based and newer visit types were running at a meaningfully lower capture rate than core inpatient, and nobody had looked at it because the volume was smaller. At scale, the revenue drag was significant.

They weren't understaffed. The coders were experienced. It was a workflow and throughput problem, not a headcount problem.

That's the part most VP Finance teams miss until they run the benchmark. Does that pattern sound familiar at Saint Luke's?

Hello Amy,

One thing I didn't mention: after they fixed the coding layer, the hospital-at-home program hit budget-neutrality six months earlier than projected. That changed the CMS conversation entirely.

The full picture: coding costs down 40%, A/R days down 30% in the first quarter, denials down 27%. The coders processing 500 charts per day at 98%+ accuracy across visit types, including the newer ones that had been dragging.

For a program that needs to prove itself financially, that's the lever most teams overlook until it's already costing them.

Would it be useful to run the same benchmark against what Saint Luke's is seeing today?
